explanation: The statement states that 'if prop_0, then prop_1'. In LTL, to specify this for every moment in time, we use 'Globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 also holds.' This ensures that at every time step, whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must be true as well.

The requirement states that whenever prop_0 holds, the controller must not be able to prop_1 at that time. This is a safety property, ensuring that at any point, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is false.
The requirement states that whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' should not hold at the same time. This can be captured as: globally, if 'prop_0' is true, then 'prop_1' must be false.

The sentence states that if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds as well. This is a straightforward implication at any instant in time. In LTL, this is usually expressed globally, meaning it holds at all times: if prop_0 holds at any moment, prop_1 must also hold at that moment.
The property states that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at that same time. This is a standard safety property, usually encoded as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.
This specification expresses that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must not hold at the same time; i.e., if 'prop_0' is true, 'prop_1' should be false.

The requirement states that if 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' should not be possible. In temporal logic, this is usually interpreted as 'globally, whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 does not hold at the same time'. This can be directly translated to 'G (prop_0 -> !prop_1)'.

The requirement states that if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 should also hold. In LTL, this can be represented as 'G (prop_0 -> prop_1)', which means: globally (at all times), if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be true as well.

If prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold, globally. This expresses that at every point in time, whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 is also true. This is captured in LTL as a globally (G) statement using implication.

The statement is a conditional: when prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, the always (G) operator is used to enforce this at all times: globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be true as well.
